docs.title: Revelation Grant-as-CodeOne system for community, grants, and delivery.
docs.text:
Intro Mission Model Flow Tracks Roadmap Join

smartmur collective

Revelation Grant-as-Code turns lived experience into an operating model: community evidence, clear proposals, practical program design, and measurable outcomes.

See the Mission View Operating Flow Mission

Build economic pathways for immigrant mothers through community-led program design, transparent grant workflows, and accessible technology.

Community First

Start with focus groups, surveys, and partner input before building anything.

Grant-as-Code

Version every proposal, review every change, and keep institutional memory visible.

Outcome Focus

Track real movement in confidence, employment, and income.

What The System Includes Foundation

Vision, governance, and roadmap to keep decisions aligned.

Open Foundation Docs Community Ops

Advisory structure, partner strategy, and contribution pathways.

Open Community Docs Community Assessment

Protocols, findings, and evidence that shape the design.

Open Community Docs Program Design

Cohort model, curriculum flow, and support structure.

Open Program Docs Pilot Preparation

Enrollment, staffing, logistics, and launch-readiness gates.

Open Pilot Docs Pilot Execution

Weekly delivery, support loops, job preparation, and outcome tracking.

Open Execution Docs Refine and Scale

Year-2 refinement, sustainability planning, expansion, and open release prep.

Open Scale Docs Launch Activation

Week-by-week launch sprint execution from go-live through first submission and momentum.

Open Launch Docs Reporting and Renewal

Post-launch monthly and quarterly reporting cadence with renewal pipeline operations.

Open Reporting Docs Impact Model

Indicators and theory-of-change logic for reporting.

Open Impact Docs Grant Operations

Research list, templates, active pipeline, and awarded archive.

Open Grant Docs Communications

Website, one-pager, press kit, and social content mapped to delivery goals.

Open Communications Docs Technology

Grant OS Lite architecture with mobile-first constraints.

Open Technology Docs Operating Flow
  1. Listen

    Capture barriers, aspirations, and constraints from the community.

  2. Design

    Convert evidence into program structure and grant narrative.

  3. Ship

    Deliver docs, workflows, and implementation through review gates.

  4. Measure

    Track outcomes and feed insights into the next cycle.

Current Tracks Community Track

Focus groups, synthesis, and partner validation.

Grant Track

Opportunity mapping and proposal template hardening.

Platform Track

Grant OS Lite architecture and accessibility baseline.

Scale Track

Phase 7 evaluation synthesis, funding diversification, and expansion readiness.

Communications Track

Phase 9 narrative refresh, publishing cadence, and media outreach execution.

Launch Track

Phase 10 launch checklist execution, funder meetings, submissions, and momentum loops.

Renewal Track

Phase 12 renewal submissions, shared annex operations, and funder follow-up cadence.

Roadmap Snapshot

Phase 0

Foundation

Repo setup, governance, workflows, docs baseline.

Phase 1

Community Grounding

Focus groups, survey deployment, partner commitments.

Phase 2-4

Design + Build

Program design, grant submissions, and technology MVP.

Phase 5-6

Pilot Delivery

Cohort launch, support loops, and measured outcomes.

Phase 7

Refine + Scale

Comprehensive evaluation, funding diversification, and multi-site expansion planning.

Phase 8-10

Harden + Launch

Product hardening, communications execution, and launch sprint activation.

Phase 11

Report + Renew

Monthly/quarterly evidence reporting, funder updates, and renewal-cycle preparation (complete).

Phase 12

Submit + Follow Up

Shared annex reuse, renewal package submissions, and logged follow-up execution.

Get Involved

Contribute as a community member, partner, grant writer, developer, or reviewer.

docs.title: How We Categorize What We Hear
🎯 Core Categories
docs.text: Code Description Example BARRIER Obstacles to learning or working "I can't afford childcare" MOTIVATION Reasons they want to learn "I want my daughter to be proud" SUPPORT What would help them succeed "If I had a mentor..." FEAR Anxieties about technology "I'll break the computer" HOPE Dreams and aspirations "I want my own business" TIME Availability constraints "Only when kids are in school" LANGUAGE Language-related challenges "English is hard for me" COMMUNITY Desire for connection "I want to meet other moms" PAST EXPERIENCE Previous learning attempts "I tried online but quit" FAMILY Family dynamics and support "My husband doesn't understand"

docs.title: BARRIER Sub-codes
docs.text: Code Description BARRIER-CHILDCARE No childcare available BARRIER-COST Cannot afford programs/devices BARRIER-TIME No time due to responsibilities BARRIER-CONFIDENCE Don't believe they can learn BARRIER-LANGUAGE English/French barrier BARRIER-TRANSPORT Can't get to locations BARRIER-NETWORK No connections to jobs

docs.title: SUPPORT Sub-codes
docs.text: Code Description SUPPORT-CHILDCARE On-site childcare needed SUPPORT-PEERS Learning with other women SUPPORT-MENTOR One-on-one guidance SUPPORT-DEVICE Need laptop/computer SUPPORT-TRANSPORT Help getting there SUPPORT-LANGUAGE Materials in first language SUPPORT-FLEXIBLE Schedule that works for moms

docs.title: 🛠️ How to Code
docs.text:
1. Read transcript completely once
  2. Second read: Highlight quotes and assign codes
  3. Third read: Look for patterns and connections
  4. Review: Check for consistency across coders
  5. Count: Frequency of each code

docs.title: Need Statement: Draft for Grants
docs.text: Based on focus groups with 52 immigrant mothers in New York City and March 2026 intake tracking

docs.title: 🎯 For Grant Proposals
docs.text: This section can be adapted for any grant's "Problem Statement" or "Need" section. Mix and match paragraphs and quotes based on word count.

docs.title: 📊 The Data Summary
docs.text: Community: Immigrant mothers in New York City Engagement: 5 focus groups, 52 participants Languages: Arabic, Spanish, Punjabi, Tagalog, Mandarin, Tamil, English Key Finding: 84% cite childcare as primary barrier; 86% want job placement, not just training Current Cycle Check (March 2026): 5 of first 6 confirmed participants requested childcare support

docs.title: The Hidden Barriers Immigrant Mothers Face
docs.text:
Immigrant mothers arrive in Canada with resilience, determination, and dreams for their children. Yet they face overlapping barriers that keep them from economic opportunity—barriers that traditional training programs fail to address.

The Childcare Trap

For mothers of young children, attending any program requires solving an impossible equation. With family often thousands of miles away and childcare costs consuming what little income they might earn, most simply cannot participate.

"I have no one. My family is all back home. If I go somewhere, my baby comes with me or I don't go."

This reality excludes immigrant mothers from workforce development opportunities before they even begin. Of 52 women consulted, 84% identified childcare as the single greatest barrier to learning new skills.

The Confidence Gap

Strikingly, the women we spoke with already possess raw ability—most use smartphones, navigate apps, and support their children's learning. What holds them back is not capability but belief.

"I'm 45. Isn't that too old to learn computers?"

Seventy-two percent described feeling "not smart enough" or "too old" to learn technology. This confidence gap, not skills gap, is the true barrier.

The Job Connection

Women are skeptical of programs that teach skills without connecting to employment. They've seen friends complete training only to remain jobless.

"I don't want to learn just to learn. I want a job at the end."

Eighty-six percent said job placement support was essential—not optional.

The Isolation Factor

Many immigrant mothers experience profound isolation. Without family networks and cut off from professional communities, they lack both support and connections to opportunity.

"I'm alone here. No family. I want to be with women who understand."

The Access Gap

While most have smartphones, few have laptops or reliable internet for serious learning. Sixty-two percent lack adequate devices; 54% have unreliable internet.

"My husband takes the computer to work. I have nothing during the day."

The System Failure

Existing programs fail because they're designed for a different reality. Evening classes assume women have no childcare responsibilities. English-only instruction excludes those building language skills. Individual learning models ignore the power of community.

The result is a population of talented, motivated women locked out of the digital economy—not by ability, but by systems that weren't built for them.

The Opportunity

When immigrant mothers gain technology skills and economic independence, families transform. Children see their mothers working. Income increases. Communities strengthen.

But only if we design for their actual lives.

docs.title: What Immigrant Mothers Told Us
docs.text:
When we asked 52 immigrant mothers what stands between them and economic opportunity, their answers were clear—and surprising.

Childcare is the unseen barrier. Eighty-four percent said they cannot attend any program without childcare support. With family abroad and costs prohibitive, most simply don't participate in existing offerings.

"I have no one. If I go somewhere, my baby comes with me or I don't go."

Confidence, not skills, is the real gap. Women already use smartphones and apps. What holds them back is belief—72% described feeling "too old" or "not smart enough" to learn technology.

Jobs, not just training, is the goal. Eighty-six percent said job placement support is essential. They've seen too many programs promise skills and deliver no employment.

"I don't want to learn just to learn. I want a job at the end."

Existing programs don't fit their lives. Evening classes assume no childcare. English-only instruction excludes language learners. Individual models ignore the power of community.

The result: talented, motivated women locked out of the digital economy—not by ability, but by programs designed for someone else's reality.

docs.title: In Their Own Words
docs.text:
The Revelation Project began by listening. Over five focus groups, 52 immigrant mothers told us what stands between them and economic opportunity.

On childcare:

"I have no one. My family is all back home. If I go somewhere, my baby comes with me or I don't go."

On confidence:

"I'm 45. Isn't that too old to learn computers?"

"I tried once. I couldn't figure it out. I felt so dumb I never tried again."

On what they actually want:

"I don't want to learn just to learn. I want a job at the end."

"If you can connect me to real employers, I'll do anything."

On community:

"I'm alone here. No family. I want to be with women who understand."

"We need each other. That's how we survive."

On their dreams:

"I want my daughter to see me working. To be proud."

"I want to open my own business. Help other women like me."

These voices reveal a truth that data alone cannot capture: immigrant mothers possess the motivation, the resilience, and the dreams. What they lack are programs designed for their actual lives.

docs.text: Grant Section Use Problem Statement Draft 1 or 2 Community Engagement Describe focus group process Need Statement Data points + 2-3 quotes Program Design Implications from findings Appendix Full summary (optional)

docs.title: Thematic Analysis: Focus Group Findings
docs.text: Analysis Period: March 1, 2026 to March 10, 2026 Focus Groups Included: Baseline synthesis + Focus Group #1 recruitment cycle Total Participants: 52 baseline participants + 8 live-cycle recruits Analysts: Community Ops Team

docs.title: 📋 Executive Summary
docs.text: Analysis from baseline focus groups and the first 2026 recruitment cycle confirms that childcare, confidence, and job relevance remain the highest-leverage design factors. Live tracker data shows childcare support requests in 5 of the first 6 confirmed participants, validating that participation logistics still determine access. Morning scheduling remains the most viable default with evening overflow slots for specific family schedules. Program planning should keep language interpretation, peer-cohort structures, and employer-linked outcomes as core requirements rather than optional add-ons.

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 1: Childcare Is the Unseen Barrier
What We Heard
docs.text:
Across all groups, childcare emerged as the single biggest barrier to participating in any program. Women described:

  • No family nearby to help
  • Childcare costs equal to what they'd earn
  • Programs offered only during school hours (excluding mothers of preschoolers)
  • Guilt about leaving children

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I have no one. My family is all back home. If I go somewhere, my baby comes with me or I don't go." — Participant 3, Group 1

"I looked at a program but it cost $50 for childcare each time. That's more than I would make." — Participant 7, Group 2

"Evenings? That's when I put them to bed. By 8pm I'm asleep too." — Participant 2, Group 3

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % of Participants BARRIER-CHILDCARE 42 84% SUPPORT-CHILDCARE 38 76%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Must provide on-site childcare during all sessions
  • Schedule during school hours for mothers of school-age children
  • Consider younger children - separate room with qualified care
  • Budget for childcare provider as core program cost

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 2: Confidence, Not Skills, Is the Real Gap
What We Heard
docs.text: Women consistently described feeling "not smart enough" or "too old" to learn technology. Yet when asked about specific tasks, many already used smartphones, WhatsApp, and basic apps. The gap wasn't ability—it was belief.

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I'm 45. Isn't that too old to learn computers?" — Participant 9, Group 1

"My daughter says I'm stupid with phones. Maybe she's right." — Participant 4, Group 2

"I tried once. I couldn't figure it out. I felt so dumb I never tried again." — Participant 11, Group 3

"If I saw other women like me doing it, maybe I'd believe I could." — Participant 6, Group 4

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % of Participants BARRIER-CONFIDENCE 36 72% FEAR 31 62% SUPPORT-PEERS 29 58%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Peer learning - women learn with women like them
  • Celebrate small wins - build confidence gradually
  • Representation matters - mentors who look like participants
  • No judgment zone - explicit culture of "no stupid questions"

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 3: Evenings Don't Work. Mornings Do.
What We Heard
docs.text:
Conventional wisdom says offer evening classes for working people. But immigrant mothers described a different reality:

  • Evenings are for children's homework and bedtime
  • After a full day, they're exhausted
  • Morning (during school hours) is when they have time

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"Evening? No, that's when I help with homework and cook dinner. I can't." — Participant 12, Group 1

"The best time for me is 9:30 to 2:30, when kids are in school." — Participant 5, Group 2

"Saturday mornings work because my husband is home with kids." — Participant 8, Group 3

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Time Preference Count % Weekday mornings 28 56% Saturday mornings 15 30% Weekday afternoons 12 24% Weekday evenings 4 8% Saturdays afternoons 3 6%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Offer morning sessions (9:30am-2:30pm) as primary option
  • Saturday option for women whose partners work weekends
  • Consider shorter, more frequent sessions rather than long evenings
  • Avoid evenings except as rare accommodation

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 4: Want Jobs, Not Just Skills
What We Heard
docs.text: Women aren't interested in learning for learning's sake. They want pathways to employment—and they're skeptical of programs that promise skills but don't deliver jobs.

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I don't want to learn just to learn. I want a job at the end." — Participant 2, Group 1

"My friend did a program. Now she has a certificate and still no job." — Participant 7, Group 3

"If you can connect me to real employers, I'll do anything." — Participant 10, Group 4

"I want to know: will this actually lead to money?" — Participant 1, Group 2

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % MOTIVATION-JOB 43 86% SKEPTICISM 22 44% SUPPORT-EMPLOYER 35 70%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Employer partnerships from day one
  • Job placement support as core program component
  • Realistic outcomes - don't promise what we can't deliver
  • Track and share job placement data transparently

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 5: Community Matters as Much as Curriculum
What We Heard
docs.text: Women consistently described wanting to learn with women like them. Isolation is a barrier; community is a solution.

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I'm alone here. No family. I want to be with women who understand." — Participant 6, Group 1

"If I know other women are waiting for me, I'll show up." — Participant 11, Group 2

"We can help each other. What one doesn't know, another might." — Participant 4, Group 3

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % SUPPORT-PEERS 29 58% ISOLATION 24 48% COMMUNITY 33 66%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Cohort model - women start and finish together
  • Peer circles - small groups within larger cohort
  • Social events - build relationships beyond classroom
  • WhatsApp groups - ongoing connection between sessions

docs.title: 🎯 Theme 6: Devices Are a Barrier, But Not the Main One
What We Heard
docs.text: While many women have smartphones, few have laptops or reliable internet for serious learning. But devices without support don't help.

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I have a phone but it's old. Can't watch videos." — Participant 8, Group 1

"My husband takes the computer to work. I have nothing during the day." — Participant 3, Group 2

"The internet at home is slow. We share with three other families." — Participant 9, Group 4

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % BARRIER-DEVICE 31 62% BARRIER-INTERNET 27 54% SUPPORT-DEVICE 34 68%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Provide laptops for participants to keep
  • Hotspots or internet subsidy for home access
  • On-site devices for in-person sessions
  • Low-bandwidth options for all online materials

docs.title: What We Heard
docs.text: Women's English/French levels vary widely. Those with limited proficiency feel excluded from most programs.

docs.title: Key Quotes
docs.text:
"I understand but I'm scared to speak. What if I say wrong?" — Participant 12, Group 3

"If there was someone who spoke my language, I would come." — Participant 5, Group 1

"I want to learn English AND computers at same time." — Participant 7, Group 2

docs.title: Frequency
docs.text: Code Count % BARRIER-LANGUAGE 28 56% SUPPORT-LANGUAGE 32 64%

docs.title: Implications for Program Design
docs.text:
• Multilingual materials - key documents in multiple languages
  • Interpretation available for all sessions
  • Bilingual facilitators where possible
  • Language learning integrated with tech learning

docs.title: What Challenged Our Assumptions
docs.text: Assumption Reality Implication Evenings would work best Mornings are preferred Adjust schedule Smartphones are enough Need laptops for serious learning Include device provision Skills are main gap Confidence is bigger barrier Focus on mindset Individual learning is fine Community is essential Cohort model critical

docs.title: 📝 Implications Summary
docs.text: Finding Program Response Childcare is #1 barrier On-site childcare, school-hour schedule Confidence gap Peer learning, celebration of wins Mornings preferred 9:30am-2:30pm sessions Jobs, not just skills Employer partnerships, placement support Community matters Cohort model, peer circles Device gap Laptops provided, hotspots Language support Interpretation, multilingual materials

docs.title: Sustainability Plan: Revelation Grant-as-Code
docs.text: Version: 1.2 Date: 2026-03-10 Approved by: Advisory Committee (pending next quarterly review) Review Cadence: Quarterly

docs.title: Vision
docs.text: The project is designed to outlast any single grant and transition toward community-led continuity. Sustainability here means financial resilience, delivery quality at scale, and local ownership.

docs.title: Sustainability Framework
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph Financial
        A1[Government and Foundation Grants]
        A2[Corporate Sponsorships]
        A3[Earned Revenue]
        A4[Individual Donations]
    end

    subgraph Operational
        B1[Partner MOUs and Delivery Standards]
        B2[Staffing and Facilitator Pipeline]
        B3[Technology and Documentation Infrastructure]
        B4[Risk Monitoring Cadence]
    end

    subgraph Community
        C1[Advisory Participation]
        C2[Participant-to-Mentor Pathways]
        C3[Train-the-Trainer Model]
        C4[Open Community of Practice]
    end

    Financial --> DurableProgram[Durable Program]
    Operational --> DurableProgram
    Community --> DurableProgram

docs.title: Revenue Diversification Targets
docs.text: Source Year 2 Target Year 3 Target Year 5 Target Rationale Government and foundation grants 55% 45% 35% Maintain core stability while reducing concentration risk Corporate sponsorships 20% 25% 30% Build predictable annual partnerships tied to talent pipeline value Earned revenue 15% 20% 25% Grow services that reinforce program outcomes Individual donors 10% 10% 10% Sustain unrestricted support for participant-critical services

docs.title: Guardrails
docs.text:
• No single revenue source exceeds 40% in Year 2 and 35% by Year 3.
  • Childcare, interpretation, and participant technology access remain protected budget lines.
  • Operating reserve target: minimum 3 months by end of Year 3.

docs.title: Earned Revenue Strategy
docs.text: Stream Description Year 2 Goal Risk Control Employer readiness training Paid workshops on inclusive onboarding for newcomer women talent 4 pilot workshops Keep delivery separate from participant support capacity Implementation consulting Support partners adopting the open program package 2 pilot engagements Use fixed-scope statements and documented templates Program materials support Optional service package for implementation coaching 3 paid adopters Preserve free core materials; charge only for support services

docs.title: Corporate Sponsorship Model
docs.text: Tier Annual Range Benefits Tech Partner 5,000 to 10,000 USD Brand placement, two employee mentors, partner updates Innovation Partner 10,000 to 25,000 USD Above plus hiring events and outcomes briefings Presenting Partner 25,000 to 50,000 USD Above plus cohort sponsorship and executive engagement

docs.title: Community Ownership and Capacity
docs.text:
• Maintain advisory composition where immigrant mothers hold majority representation.
  • Build alumni pathway into mentorship and facilitator preparation.
  • Operate a train-the-trainer pathway for partner organizations before second-city launch.
  • Keep all operating assets versioned and openly documented for continuity.

docs.title: Risk and Mitigation
docs.text:
Primary risks are tracked in community/scale/sustainability-risk-register.csv with monthly review.

Risk Theme Leading Indicator Mitigation Trigger Funding concentration Any source exceeds target threshold Shift proposal and sponsorship mix in next cycle Placement capacity constraints Declining employer partner engagement Run partner reactivation campaign and expand prospect list Facilitator capacity constraints Fewer than four certified facilitators for expansion window Activate remediation and standby roster before enrollment opens Delivery quality drift Support SLA misses or lower attendance Freeze expansion steps until staffing and support KPIs recover

docs.title: Origin Story
docs.text: This project began with a practical problem: motivated learners faced structural barriers to accessing technology pathways.

docs.title: Core Beliefs
docs.text:
1. Knowledge should be open and reusable.
  2. Community should shape program design.
  3. Lived experience is a source of expertise.
  4. Economic independence changes family outcomes.
  5. Transparent operations build trust.

docs.title: North Star
docs.text: By 2028, 500 immigrant mothers achieve economic independence through technology career pathways, with open tools and curriculum that others can reuse.

docs.title: Commitments
docs.text:
1. Listen before building.
  2. Pay community contributors.
  3. Start small, then scale.
  4. Open source core materials.
  5. Track outcomes, not just output volume.

docs.text: • Evaluation Framework

docs.title: Evaluation Framework: Revelation Project
docs.text: Version: 1.1 Date: March 10, 2026 Status: Draft for March 24, 2026 advisory approval Evaluation Lead: Program Operations and Learning External Partner: Pending (university and community research partner outreach in progress)

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This framework defines how the project will measure delivery quality and participant outcomes across the pilot cycle.

docs.title: Evaluation Principles
docs.text: Principle Application Community-led Advisory and participants review tools before use. Useful first Metrics must drive operational decisions, not only reporting. Proportionate Data collection burden stays low for participants. Ethical Consent, data minimization, and de-identification are required. Transparent Aggregated findings are shared in project documentation.

docs.title: Core Questions
docs.text:
1. Are participants able to access and stay in the program?
  2. Are supports removing major barriers (childcare, language, devices, transport)?
  3. Are participants building confidence and job-readiness?
  4. Are outcomes trending toward employment and income mobility?

docs.title: Indicator Set
docs.text: Area Indicator Target Access Enrollment to attendance conversion >= 75% Access Participants requiring childcare who receive it 100% Access Participants requiring interpretation who receive it 100% Engagement Session attendance rate >= 80% Learning Resources completed per participant >= 8 core modules Confidence Self-rated confidence change (pre/post) +40% median improvement Employment Job interview rate within 3 months >= 60% Employment Placement within 6 months >= 70%

docs.title: Data Sources
docs.text: Source Frequency Owner Recruitment tracker Weekly Community Ops Attendance and session logs Per session Program Team Resource completion logs Weekly Program Team Pre/post participant surveys Start and end of cohort Learning Lead Follow-up outcomes survey 3 and 6 months post-program Alumni and Ops

docs.title: Collection Cadence
docs.text:
1. Weekly operations dashboard update.
  2. Biweekly learning review with program team.
  3. Monthly advisory review of trends and risks.
  4. Quarterly public summary update (anonymized).

docs.title: Data Governance
docs.text:
• Participant files use internal IDs, not full names, in analysis reports.
  • Consent status is required before survey or quote publication.
  • Raw notes and recordings remain access-restricted.
  • Public artifacts contain aggregated or anonymized data only.

docs.title: Reporting Outputs
docs.text: Output Audience Frequency Operations dashboard Internal team Weekly Advisory packet Advisory committee Monthly Grant evidence summary Funders and grant writers Per submission cycle Public impact snapshot Community and partners Quarterly

docs.text:

  • Review indicator definitions during advisory kickoff on March 24, 2026.
  • Run first dashboard cycle after Focus Group #1 debrief.

docs.title: Program Design: Version 1
docs.text: Based on community assessment findings - March 10, 2026

docs.title: 🎯 Design Principles
docs.text: Principle Source Community-led Advisory committee (51% immigrant mothers) Barrier-first Address childcare, devices, confidence before curriculum Job-connected Employer partnerships from day one Peer-powered Learning happens in community Flexible Designed for mothers' actual schedules Transparent Open source, publicly documented

docs.title: 👥 Participant Profile
docs.text:
Characteristic Target Who Immigrant mothers (any country) Children Any age (childcare provided) Time in Canada 0-10 years Current work Unemployed or underemployed Tech experience Any level, including none Language All languages (interpretation provided) Income Low-income (program free)

Cohort size: 15-20 women

docs.title: Duration: 6 months
docs.text: Phase Duration Focus Phase 1: Foundations Months 1-2 Digital literacy, confidence building, peer connections Phase 2: Skills Months 3-4 Technical skills (web development, data, or design) Phase 3: Job Readiness Month 5 Resume, interviews, employer connections Phase 4: Placement Month 6 Job search support, mentorship, alumni network

docs.title: 🗓️ Weekly Schedule
docs.text:
Primary Option: Weekday Mornings - Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday - 9:30am - 2:30pm (5 hours) - Includes: instruction, practice, lunch, peer time

Secondary Option: Saturdays - Saturday mornings - 9:30am - 1:30pm (4 hours) - For women whose partners work weekends

Why this schedule? 84% of participants preferred weekday mornings; only 8% preferred evenings.

docs.title: 1. Childcare (On-site)
docs.text:
Detail Description Ages 0-5 years (separate room), 6-12 years (homework help) Staff Qualified early childhood educators Ratio 1:3 for infants, 1:5 for toddlers, 1:8 for school-age Schedule Same as program hours Cost Free to participants

Source: 84% identified childcare as primary barrier

docs.title: 2. Device Provision
docs.text:
Item Description Laptop Refurbished or new laptop for each participant Ownership Yours to keep after program completion Hotspot Mobile hotspot or internet subsidy Support Basic tech support throughout program

Source: 62% lack adequate devices

docs.title: 3. Peer Cohorts
docs.text:
Element Description Cohort size 15-20 women start and finish together Peer circles Small groups (4-5) within cohort WhatsApp groups Ongoing connection between sessions Social events Monthly community gatherings

Source: 58% want peer learning; 48% experience isolation

docs.title: 4. Mentorship
docs.text:
Element Description Mentors Women in tech (immigrant background preferred) Match 1 mentor per 3-4 participants Frequency Monthly group mentoring + optional 1:1 Focus Career guidance, confidence, network

Source: 72% confidence gap

docs.title: 5. Employer Partnerships
docs.text:
Element Description Partners 5-10 committed employers Involvement Guest talks, site visits, job shadows Hiring Pipeline for program graduates Support Ongoing support for employers and graduates

Source: 86% want job placement, not just training

docs.title: 6. Language Support
docs.text:
Element Description Interpretation Available for all sessions Materials Key documents in multiple languages Facilitators Bilingual where possible Language learning Integrated English/French support

Source: 56% language barrier

docs.title: Phase 1: Foundations (Weeks 1-8)
docs.text: Week Focus Output 1 Welcome, community building, tech setup Cohort connected 2 Digital basics: files, folders, internet Comfort with computer 3 Online safety, passwords, privacy Safe practices 4 Email, communication tools Professional email 5 Introduction to web How internet works 6 Career exploration Interest identified 7 Confidence building Personal story 8 Phase 1 celebration Portfolio start

docs.title: Phase 2: Skills (Weeks 9-16)
docs.text:
Participants choose one track

Track A: Web Development - HTML/CSS basics - Building simple websites - JavaScript fundamentals - Project: Personal portfolio site

Track B: Data - Spreadsheets fundamentals - Data visualization - Introduction to SQL - Project: Data analysis portfolio

Track C: Digital Design - Canva and design basics - Social media content - Introduction to UX - Project: Design portfolio

docs.title: Phase 3: Job Readiness (Weeks 17-20)
docs.text: Week Focus 17 Resume and LinkedIn workshop 18 Interview practice (with employers) 19 Job search strategies 20 Networking event with employers

docs.title: Phase 4: Placement (Weeks 21-24)
docs.text:
• Supported job applications
  • Employer introductions
  • Interview coaching
  • Job offer support
  • Alumni network entry

docs.title: 🔒 Scope Lock Decisions (Q2 2026)
docs.text:
The advisory pre-read and operations review on March 10, 2026 produced a scoped 90-day build window:

  1. Launch one pilot focus-group sequence before adding additional tracks.
  2. Keep one primary schedule: weekday mornings, with one evening overflow slot.
  3. Treat childcare, interpretation, and transportation support as baseline budget lines.
  4. Prioritize web and workforce readiness modules before adding advanced specializations.
  5. Gate grant submissions through a shared evaluation and readiness checklist.

Reference: docs/program/scope-lock-q2-2026.md

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
1. Accessibility first
  2. Privacy by design
  3. Simple and maintainable stack
  4. Community ownership
  5. Iterative delivery

docs.title: Current Runtime Architecture
docs.text:
• Frontend: server-rendered HTML templates with lightweight CSS
  • Backend: Flask application with blueprint-based routing
  • State: in-memory demo data store for rapid iteration (src/grant-os/app/data.py)
  • Auth: session-based sign-in/sign-out for participant route protection

docs.title: Implemented MVP Features
docs.text:
1. Authenticated participant flow (/auth/login, /auth/logout)
  2. Participant dashboard with sessions and learning snapshot
  3. Resource library with completion tracking
  4. Peer-circle discussion view with message posting
  5. End-to-end smoke test for key user flows

docs.title: Planned Hardening Path
docs.text:
• Move demo in-memory store to PostgreSQL-backed persistence.
  • Introduce role-aware access control for staff, mentors, and participants.
  • Add audit logging and stronger credential management.
  • Add API surface for external partner integrations.

docs.title: Security and Privacy
docs.text:
• Session secret managed via environment variable for non-local environments.
  • Data minimization in demo environment (no sensitive participant identifiers committed).
  • Consent-based sharing model defined in program and evaluation docs.
  • Compliance review required before any production deployment.
